content_html:
<html:div xmlns:html="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml">
<html:p class="ai-disclaimer">Written by me, proof-read by an LLM.
<html:br />Details at end.</html:p>

<html:p><html:a href="/202512/20-simd-city">Yesterday</html:a> we saw SIMD work beautifully with integers. But floating point has a surprise in store. Let's try summing an array<html:sup id="fnref:sum"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:sum">1</html:a></html:sup>:</html:p>

<html:p>Looking at the core loop, the compiler has pulled off a clever trick:</html:p>

<html:div class="codehilite"><html:pre><html:span /><html:code><html:span class="nl">.L2:</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="c1">; pick up 8 integers and add them element-wise to ymm0</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">vpaddd</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">ymm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">ymm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">YMMWORD</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">PTR</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rdi</html:span><html:span class="p">]</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">add</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rdi</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="mi">32</html:span><html:span class="w">       </html:span><html:span class="c1">; move to the next batch of integers</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">cmp</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rax</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rdi</html:span><html:span class="w">      </html:span><html:span class="c1">; at the end?</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">jne</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">.L2</html:span><html:span class="w">           </html:span><html:span class="c1">; loop if not</html:span>
</html:code></html:pre></html:div>


<html:p>The compiler is using a vectorised add instruction which treats the <html:code>ymm0</html:code> as 8 separate integers, adding them up individually to the corresponding elements read from <html:code>[rdi]</html:code>. That's incredibly efficient, processing 8 integers per loop iteration, but at the end of that loop we'll have 8 separate subtotals. The compiler adds a bit of "fix up" code after the loop to sum all these 8 subtotals:</html:p>

<html:div class="codehilite"><html:pre><html:span /><html:code><html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">vextracti128</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm1</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">ymm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="mi">0x1</html:span><html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="c1">; xmm1 = ymm0 >> 128</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">vpaddd</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm1</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="w">       </html:span><html:span class="c1">; xmm0 += xmm1</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">vpsrldq</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm1</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="mi">8</html:span><html:span class="w">         </html:span><html:span class="c1">; xmm1 = xmm0 >> 64</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">vpaddd</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm1</html:span><html:span class="w">       </html:span><html:span class="c1">; xmm0 += xmm1</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">vpsrldq</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm1</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="mi">4</html:span><html:span class="w">         </html:span><html:span class="c1">; xmm1 = xmm0 >> 32</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">vpaddd</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm1</html:span><html:span class="w">       </html:span><html:span class="c1">; xmm0 += xmm1</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">vmovd</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">eax</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="w">               </html:span><html:span class="c1">; return xmm0</html:span>
</html:code></html:pre></html:div>


<html:p>This sequence repeatedly adds the "top half" of the result to the bottom half until there's only one left. This fix up code is a little bit extra work, but the efficiency of the loop makes up for it<html:sup id="fnref:esp"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:esp">2</html:a></html:sup>.</html:p>

<html:p>Let's switch to <html:code>float</html:code>s<html:sup id="fnref:hmm"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:hmm">3</html:a></html:sup> and see what happens:</html:p>

<html:p>We're still processing 32 bytes' worth of <html:code>float</html:code>s per loop iteration<html:sup id="fnref:cheat"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:cheat">4</html:a></html:sup>, but something unfortunate is going on:</html:p>

<html:div class="codehilite"><html:pre><html:span /><html:code><html:span class="nl">.L2:</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">vaddss</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">DWORD</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">PTR</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rdi</html:span><html:span class="p">]</html:span><html:span class="w">    </html:span><html:span class="c1">; xmm0 += first elem</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">add</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rdi</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="mi">32</html:span><html:span class="w">                           </html:span><html:span class="c1">; move to next 8 floats</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">vaddss</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">DWORD</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">PTR</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rdi-28</html:span><html:span class="p">]</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="c1">; xmm0 += second</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">vaddss</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">DWORD</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">PTR</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rdi-24</html:span><html:span class="p">]</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="c1">; etc</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">vaddss</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">DWORD</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">PTR</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rdi-20</html:span><html:span class="p">]</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="c1">; ...</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">vaddss</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">DWORD</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">PTR</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rdi-16</html:span><html:span class="p">]</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">vaddss</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">DWORD</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">PTR</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rdi-12</html:span><html:span class="p">]</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">vaddss</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">DWORD</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">PTR</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rdi-8</html:span><html:span class="p">]</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">vaddss</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">xmm0</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">DWORD</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">PTR</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="p">[</html:span><html:span class="no">rdi-4</html:span><html:span class="p">]</html:span><html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="c1">; xmm0 += eighth</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">cmp</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rax</html:span><html:span class="p">,</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">rdi</html:span><html:span class="w">                          </html:span><html:span class="c1">; at the end?</html:span>
<html:span class="w">  </html:span><html:span class="nf">jne</html:span><html:span class="w"> </html:span><html:span class="no">.L2</html:span><html:span class="w">                               </html:span><html:span class="c1">; loop if not</html:span>
</html:code></html:pre></html:div>


<html:p>The compiler has chosen to do 8 floats per loop iteration...but then proceeded to do each add individually. What has happened here?</html:p>

<html:p>To understand we need to recall that maths on <html:code>float</html:code>s is special: Operations are not associative. When adding integers, you can regroup the operations however you like—<html:code>(x + y) + z</html:code> gives the same result as <html:code>x + (y + z)</html:code>. Not so with <html:code>float</html:code>s: after each operation, rounding occurs. Depending on the relative magnitudes of <html:code>x</html:code>, <html:code>y</html:code> and <html:code>z</html:code>, regrouping the additions will change the result.</html:p>

<html:p>When our compiler rewrote our integer sum loop into eight subtotals to take advantage of SIMD, and then summed the subtotals, it was regrouping the additions. We wrote a loop that summed from the first element to the last, and the compiler changed it to "accumulate every 8th element in 8 separate groups, then add those together". That works for integers but not floating point numbers.</html:p>

<html:p>So - are we stuck? Perhaps we know we don't care about associativity? Some of you may be itching to use the <html:code>-Ofast</html:code> or <html:code>-funsafe-math-optimizations</html:code><html:sup id="fnref:fun"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:fun">5</html:a></html:sup> flag here.  Both flags give the compiler leeway to globally ignore the rules about floating-point maths and both will work<html:sup id="fnref:global"><html:a class="footnote-ref" href="#fn:global">6</html:a></html:sup>. However, there's a better, more targeted way:</html:p>

<html:p>In this example, we tell GCC to "assume maths is associative" for just the function <html:code>sum</html:code> by using an annotation. That means the effects are limited to just that one function, and will be applied consistently regardless of how this function is compiled or used. Yes; it does rely on a GCC extension, which is unfortunate. In theory we can use <html:a href="https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/algorithm/reduce.html"><html:code>std::reduce</html:code></html:a> with an unsequenced execution policy, but in my testing that didn't work here.</html:p>

<html:p>Floating point maths has some unusual and perhaps surprising gotchas. Knowing about them can help you work with the compiler to get super fast vectorised code.</html:p>

<html:p><html:em>See <html:a href="https://youtu.be/lUTvi_96-D8">the video</html:a> that accompanies this post.</html:em></html:p>

<html:hr />

<html:p><html:em>This post is day 21 of <html:a href="/AoCO2025-archive">Advent of Compiler Optimisations 2025</html:a>,
a 25-day series exploring how compilers transform our code.</html:em></html:p>

<html:p><html:em>← <html:a href="/202512/20-simd-city">SIMD City: Auto-vectorisation</html:a> | <html:a href="/202512/22-memory-cunningness">Clever memory tricks</html:a> →</html:em></html:p>

<html:p><html:em>This post was written by a human (<html:a href="/MattGodbolt">Matt Godbolt</html:a>) and reviewed and proof-read by LLMs and humans.</html:em></html:p>

<html:p><html:em>Support Compiler Explorer on <html:a href="https://patreon.com/c/mattgodbolt">Patreon</html:a>
or <html:a href="https://github.com/sponsors/compiler-explorer">GitHub</html:a>,
or by buying CE products in the <html:a href="https://shop.compiler-explorer.com">Compiler Explorer Shop</html:a></html:em>.</html:p>

<html:div class="footnote">
<html:hr />
<html:ol>
<html:li id="fn:sum">
<html:p>Yes, I know, this is what <html:code>std::accumulate</html:code> and similar are for, and you'll see similar code generation if you use them. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:sum" title="Jump back to footnote 1 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
<html:li id="fn:esp">
<html:p>Especially in this situation where the compiler can see we have 65536 elements to process. If we had a variable number of elements, the compiler might add code to conditionally vectorise based on the number - which it has to do anyway to check if there are fewer than 8 elements, anyway. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:esp" title="Jump back to footnote 2 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
<html:li id="fn:hmm">
<html:p>If you go back to yesterday's post and switch to <html:code>float</html:code>s, you'll see the compiler is able to <html:code>max</html:code> with vectors just as well as integers. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:hmm" title="Jump back to footnote 3 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
<html:li id="fn:cheat">
<html:p>My heuristic to see if the compiler has vectorised is to find the loop and then look at how much the compiler adds to the loop counter: it's a good starting point for telling if we're doing more than one element at a time. In this case though, it's slightly misleading. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:cheat" title="Jump back to footnote 4 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
<html:li id="fn:fun">
<html:p>My favourite flag to the compiler, though it is neither fun nor safe. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:fun" title="Jump back to footnote 5 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
<html:li id="fn:global">
<html:p>You can edit the flags in the example and see it work. However, both affect the program/compiled translation unit globally. That can lead to some unfortunate side effects outside of the one or two functions you <html:em>actually</html:em> want this applied to. Chandler Carruth refers to this as "unbounded precision loss" and he's not wrong. <html:a class="footnote-backref" href="#fnref:global" title="Jump back to footnote 6 in the text">↩</html:a></html:p>
</html:li>
</html:ol>
</html:div>

    </html:div>
content_text: Written by me, proof-read by an LLM. Details at end. Yesterday we saw SIMD work beautifully with integers. But floating point has a surprise in store. Let's try summing an array 1 : Looking at the core loop, the compiler has pulled off a clever trick: .L2: ; pick up 8 integers and add them element-wise to ymm0 vpaddd ymm0 , ymm0 , YMMWORD PTR [ rdi ] add rdi , 32 ; move to the next batch of integers cmp rax , rdi ; at the end? jne .L2 ; loop if not The compiler is using a vectorised add instruction which treats the ymm0 as 8 separate integers, adding them up individually to the corresponding elements read from [rdi] . That's incredibly efficient, processing 8 integers per loop iteration, but at the end of that loop we'll have 8 separate subtotals. The compiler adds a bit of "fix up" code after the loop to sum all these 8 subtotals: vextracti128 xmm1 , ymm0 , 0x1 ; xmm1 = ymm0 >> 128 vpaddd xmm0 , xmm1 , xmm0 ; xmm0 += xmm1 vpsrldq xmm1 , xmm0 , 8 ; xmm1 = xmm0 >> 64 vpaddd xmm0 , xmm0 , xmm1 ; xmm0 += xmm1 vpsrldq xmm1 , xmm0 , 4 ; xmm1 = xmm0 >> 32 vpaddd xmm0 , xmm0 , xmm1 ; xmm0 += xmm1 vmovd eax , xmm0 ; return xmm0 This sequence repeatedly adds the "top half" of the result to the bottom half until there's only one left. This fix up code is a little bit extra work, but the efficiency of the loop makes up for it 2 . Let's switch to float s 3 and see what happens: We're still processing 32 bytes' worth of float s per loop iteration 4 , but something unfortunate is going on: .L2: vaddss xmm0 , xmm0 , DWORD PTR [ rdi ] ; xmm0 += first elem add rdi , 32 ; move to next 8 floats vaddss xmm0 , xmm0 , DWORD PTR [ rdi-28 ] ; xmm0 += second vaddss xmm0 , xmm0 , DWORD PTR [ rdi-24 ] ; etc vaddss xmm0 , xmm0 , DWORD PTR [ rdi-20 ] ; ... vaddss xmm0 , xmm0 , DWORD PTR [ rdi-16 ] vaddss xmm0 , xmm0 , DWORD PTR [ rdi-12 ] vaddss xmm0 , xmm0 , DWORD PTR [ rdi-8 ] vaddss xmm0 , xmm0 , DWORD PTR [ rdi-4 ] ; xmm0 += eighth cmp rax , rdi ; at the end? jne .L2 ; loop if not The compiler has chosen to do 8 floats per loop iteration...but then proceeded to do each add individually. What has happened here? To understand we need to recall that maths on float s is special: Operations are not associative. When adding integers, you can regroup the operations however you like— (x + y) + z gives the same result as x + (y + z) . Not so with float s: after each operation, rounding occurs. Depending on the relative magnitudes of x , y and z , regrouping the additions will change the result. When our compiler rewrote our integer sum loop into eight subtotals to take advantage of SIMD, and then summed the subtotals, it was regrouping the additions. We wrote a loop that summed from the first element to the last, and the compiler changed it to "accumulate every 8th element in 8 separate groups, then add those together". That works for integers but not floating point numbers. So - are we stuck? Perhaps we know we don't care about associativity? Some of you may be itching to use the -Ofast or -funsafe-math-optimizations 5 flag here. Both flags give the compiler leeway to globally ignore the rules about floating-point maths and both will work 6 . However, there's a better, more targeted way: In this example, we tell GCC to "assume maths is associative" for just the function sum by using an annotation. That means the effects are limited to just that one function, and will be applied consistently regardless of how this function is compiled or used. Yes; it does rely on a GCC extension, which is unfortunate. In theory we can use std::reduce with an unsequenced execution policy, but in my testing that didn't work here. Floating point maths has some unusual and perhaps surprising gotchas. Knowing about them can help you work with the compiler to get super fast vectorised code. See the video that accompanies this post. This post is day 21 of Advent of Compiler Optimisations 2025 , a 25-day series exploring how compilers transform our code. ← SIMD City: Auto-vectorisation | Clever memory tricks → This post was written by a human ( Matt Godbolt ) and reviewed and proof-read by LLMs and humans. Support Compiler Explorer on Patreon or GitHub , or by buying CE products in the Compiler Explorer Shop . Yes, I know, this is what std::accumulate and similar are for, and you'll see similar code generation if you use them. ↩ Especially in this situation where the compiler can see we have 65536 elements to process. If we had a variable number of elements, the compiler might add code to conditionally vectorise based on the number - which it has to do anyway to check if there are fewer than 8 elements, anyway. ↩ If you go back to yesterday's post and switch to float s, you'll see the compiler is able to max with vectors just as well as integers. ↩ My heuristic to see if the compiler has vectorised is to find the loop and then look at how much the compiler adds to the loop counter: it's a good starting point for telling if we're doing more than one element at a time. In this case though, it's slightly misleading. ↩ My favourite flag to the compiler, though it is neither fun nor safe. ↩ You can edit the flags in the example and see it work. However, both affect the program/compiled translation unit globally. That can lead to some unfortunate side effects outside of the one or two functions you actually want this applied to. Chandler Carruth refers to this as "unbounded precision loss" and he's not wrong. ↩
summary: Why floating point maths doesn't vectorise like integers, and what to do about it
title: When SIMD Fails: Floating Point Associativity
